title: Methadone for Opioid Use Disorder
author: Ned

seo.title: Methadone for Opioid Use Disorder | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Methadone for Opioid Use Disorder. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
Therapeutic Effects

Methadone is an opioid agonist. It activates the brain’s opioid receptors to reduce or eliminate withdrawal symptoms and cravings. Chronic heroin use involves a rapid physical cycle progressing from withdrawal “sickness” to intoxicating “high” and back to withdrawal, often several times a day. When properly administered, methadone can create a steady state of neural activiation in which a person maintains alertness without craving or preoccupation and experiences no intoxication or withdrawal. Over time, methadone can increase a person’s tolerance to such an extent that using heroin to get “high” becomes cost prohibitive and relapse loses its incentive.

Formulations

The methadone provided through federally regulated Opioid Treatment Programs is generally administered in a liquid formulation to prevent diversion. Methadone in pill form is regularly prescribed by pain management but not addiction treatment programs.

Induction and Use

Induction generally takes place once an individual is experiencing mild to moderate withdrawal symptoms (approximately 12-24 hours after last use). A small initial dose (≤30 mg) is used for induction to prevent overdose. Federal regulations prevent a dose greater than 40mg on the first day unless it is documented in the patient’s record that 40mg was unable to suppress withdrawal symptoms. After the first day, dosing may be increased according to physician instructions. Therapeutic dosing varies by individual, but research shows doses from 60 to 100mg to be generally far more effective than lower doses.

Availability

In 1971, President Nixon ordered the establishment of a federal methadone program for the treatment of opioid use disorder. As of March 31, 2008, there were 268,071 clients receiving methadone in Opioid Treatment Programs.

Typically, persons taking methadone must go to a methadone clinic daily to receive their dose. Federal guidelines allow clients who demonstrate good compliance after nine months to take up to six doses per week home for self-administration. After two years of continuous treatment, up to one month of take home medication can be prescribed.

Because of federal and state funding for these programs, methadone is widely available to persons with opioid use disorder at little or no cost. Due to local and state policies, however, methadone treatment is not available in certain states and geographical regions.

Abuse potential

Methadone is used for two primary purposes: for the treatment of opioid use disorder and for pain management. While opioid use disorder treatment is highly controlled and regulated through federally funded Opioid Treatment Programs (OTPs), the methadone prescribed for pain management is acquired through office-based physicians for administration at home. From 1999 to 2006 the annual rate of methadone-related fatalities in the U.S. rose from 790 to 5,420. This increased rate of mortality exacerbated the stigma already associated with methadone treatment for opioid use disorder. A national assessment of methadone-related mortalities concluded that the increased fatalities were due not to the use of methadone for opioid use disorder treatment but to the increased use of methadone for pain management. Methadone treatment for opioid use disorder is far safer than the continued use of illicit substances.

Overdose Risk

Methadone taken at too high a dose or combined with other substances can cause fatal respiratory depression. For additional health risks, consult a physician.

Medically Supervised Withdrawal

The Substance Abuse and Mental Health Administration’s (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment acknowledges that, despite the risks, most persons on methadone attempt to taper from it at one or more times during their treatment. Most of these persons relapse to heroin use either during or after the tapering process. For this reason, SAMHSA identifies “medically supervised withdrawal” as an optional phase of methadone treatment, but one that should only be attempted when an individual has achieved at least two years of stability in abstinence from substances and current stability of medical and mental health, employment, legal status, and primary support system. In order to be effective, medically supervised withdrawal must performed very gradually, with small reductions of methadone, generally over the course of several months.

12-Step Compatibility

Because methadone has a significant abuse potential, persons taking methadone are often poorly received in 12-step groups. Methadone Anonymous groups have been formed to help fill the void of mutual support and 12-step programs for persons taking methadone.
